topic: A student discovers their teacher follows them on social media
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification blinked on my phone screen, a small, insistent light in the otherwise quiet afternoon. It was a direct message from Mr. Harrison. My breath hitched in my throat, the sound loud in the sudden silence of my bedroom. I stared at the screen, my thumb hovering over the reply button, unable to move. He had tagged me in a post—a picture from a school event—and then sent a private message asking about my weekend plans. My heart began to pound against my ribs, a frantic drumbeat I couldn't quiet. I quickly minimized the app, shoving the phone under my pillow as if the screen itself held a dangerous secret. I spent the next hour pacing my room, the carpet fibers feeling rough beneath my bare feet, trying to force my breathing back into a normal rhythm. Every time I glanced at my laptop, I braced myself, waiting for another digital intrusion.

[story 2]

I found the evidence tucked away in a forgotten folder on my tablet. A recent post from Ms. Evans, my history teacher, showed me sitting at a café, looking thoughtful. Beneath the photo was a comment she had left on a public forum, a comment that specifically mentioned my recent essay. A cold wash spread through my chest, making it hard to draw a full breath. I felt a sudden, intense need to check her profile again, to see if there was any context, any explanation for this unexpected connection. My hands started trembling slightly as I navigated to her page. The world seemed to narrow down to the glowing rectangle in my hands, and a strange, tight knot formed just beneath my sternum. I slowly closed the browser, the click echoing too loudly in the quiet room.

[story 3]

It happened during the mandatory online study session. I was reviewing notes when my feed refreshed, and there, pinned at the top, was a story update from Professor Davies. It was a candid shot of him smiling, captioned vaguely about 'faculty life.' Then, a direct message popped up, simple and direct: "Hope you're finding the material engaging." A wave of nausea washed over me. I dropped my pen; it clattered onto the desk, the sound sharp and final. I leaned back in my chair, gripping the arms until my knuckles turned white. My vision blurred for a moment, and I had to consciously focus on the grain of the wooden desk, anything solid. I kept my gaze fixed on the textbook, refusing to look toward the monitor where his digital presence resided.
